chunks.title: Processes and threads
chunks.text: A process is a running instance of a program with its own memory space (code, data, heap, stack). A thread is a lightweight unit of execution within a process — threads in the same process share memory but have their own stack and registers. Context switching between processes is more expensive than between threads because the OS has to swap memory mappings. Multithreading enables concurrency within one process; multiprocessing enables it across cores.

chunks.title: CPU scheduling
chunks.text: The CPU scheduler decides which ready process gets the CPU next. Common policies: FCFS (first-come-first-served, simple but causes convoy effect), SJF (shortest-job-first, optimal average wait but needs runtime prediction), Round Robin (each process gets a time quantum, good for time-sharing), Priority scheduling (highest priority runs first, risk of starvation), and Multilevel Feedback Queue (multiple queues with different priorities, used in Linux's CFS variants).

chunks.title: Synchronization and locks
chunks.text: When multiple threads access shared data, race conditions can corrupt state. A critical section is code that accesses shared data; it must execute atomically. A mutex (mutual exclusion lock) allows only one thread inside the critical section at a time. A semaphore counts available resources, supporting N concurrent accesses. Condition variables let threads wait for a predicate to become true. Improper locking causes deadlock (cycle of held locks) or starvation.

chunks.title: Deadlock
chunks.text: Deadlock occurs when a set of processes are each waiting for a resource held by another. The four Coffman conditions must all hold: mutual exclusion, hold-and-wait, no preemption, and circular wait. Strategies: prevention (break one of the conditions), avoidance (Banker's algorithm — only grant requests that keep the system in a safe state), detection and recovery (wait-for graph), or simply ignoring it (the ostrich algorithm, used in most desktop OSes).

chunks.title: Memory management and paging
chunks.text: Virtual memory gives each process the illusion of a private contiguous address space. The OS maps virtual pages to physical frames via a page table. When a process accesses a page not in RAM (page fault), the OS loads it from disk, evicting another page if needed. Replacement policies include LRU (least recently used), FIFO, and Clock (an approximation of LRU). The translation lookaside buffer (TLB) caches recent virtual-to-physical translations to speed up address resolution.

chunks.title: File systems
chunks.text: A file system organizes data on storage. Files are named byte sequences; directories are containers that map names to inodes (in Unix) or file records. The inode holds metadata (size, permissions, timestamps) and pointers to data blocks. Common file systems: ext4 (Linux, journaling), NTFS (Windows), APFS (macOS, copy-on-write). Journaling logs changes before committing them, so a crash mid-write doesn't corrupt the file system.

chunks.title: Interrupts and system calls
chunks.text: An interrupt is a signal from hardware (timer tick, disk completion, key press) that pauses the current execution and runs an interrupt handler in kernel mode. A system call is the controlled way a user-space program asks the kernel for a service (open a file, allocate memory, send a packet). System calls use software interrupts or special instructions (syscall on x86-64) to switch from user mode to kernel mode safely.

chunks.title: Inter-process communication
chunks.text: Processes don't share memory by default; the OS provides IPC mechanisms. Pipes are unidirectional byte streams between related processes. Named pipes (FIFOs) work across unrelated processes. Message queues store messages until read. Shared memory maps the same physical pages into multiple address spaces — fastest IPC but requires manual synchronization. Sockets enable IPC across machines. Signals are short asynchronous notifications like SIGINT (Ctrl+C) or SIGKILL.

chunks.title: Process states
chunks.text: A process moves through states: new (being created), ready (waiting for CPU), running (executing), waiting/blocked (waiting for I/O or event), terminated. Transitions are triggered by the scheduler (ready ↔ running), system calls (running → waiting), and events (waiting → ready). The OS keeps a Process Control Block (PCB) per process with state, registers, memory mapping, and open file descriptors.

chunks.title: Fork and exec
chunks.text: In Unix, fork() creates a new process by duplicating the caller — same code, same data, separate address spaces (copy-on-write makes this cheap). exec() replaces the current process image with a new program. The classic pattern: fork to create a child, the child execs the program, the parent waits. This separation lets shells set up file descriptors and environment before launching the new program.

chunks.title: Zombie and orphan processes
chunks.text: A zombie is a terminated child whose parent hasn't yet called wait() to collect its exit status — the kernel keeps a PCB entry. Many zombies leak PIDs. An orphan is a child whose parent died first; init (PID 1) adopts it and reaps it. Modern systemd does the same. Daemon processes deliberately orphan themselves so they're not tied to a terminal session.

chunks.title: Producer-consumer problem
chunks.text: Producers add items to a bounded buffer; consumers remove them. The classic solution uses three semaphores: mutex for buffer access, empty (initially N) decremented by producers, full (initially 0) decremented by consumers. Producers wait on empty before grabbing the mutex; consumers wait on full. Modern code often uses a condition variable on a mutex-protected queue. This is the abstract model behind any pipeline.

chunks.title: Reader-writer problem
chunks.text: Multiple readers can read concurrently, but writers need exclusive access. Implementations vary by fairness: reader-preference (writers may starve if reads keep coming), writer-preference (readers wait once a writer is queued), or fair (FIFO). pthreads provides pthread_rwlock_t with reader-preference by default. Read-heavy workloads (caches, lookup tables) benefit hugely from rwlocks over plain mutexes.

chunks.title: Dining philosophers
chunks.text: Five philosophers sit around a table; each needs the two chopsticks beside them to eat. The naive 'pick left, then right' deadlocks if everyone picks left simultaneously. Solutions: enforce ordering (pick lower-numbered first), introduce a waiter (semaphore allowing at most 4 to attempt), or use try-lock with backoff. The problem is the textbook example of deadlock arising from circular wait.

chunks.title: Demand paging and thrashing
chunks.text: Demand paging loads a page only when it's accessed, not at program start, which speeds startup and saves memory. The risk is thrashing — when active processes' working sets exceed RAM, the OS spends most time servicing page faults instead of running code. Throughput collapses. Fixes: reduce multiprogramming level, add RAM, or use working-set page replacement that keeps each process's recent pages resident.

chunks.title: Page replacement algorithms
chunks.text: When RAM is full and a new page is needed, choose a victim. FIFO is simplest but suffers Belady's anomaly (more frames can mean more faults). LRU is optimal among practical algorithms but expensive to track precisely. Clock approximates LRU using a reference bit per page and a circular scan. Optimal (Belady's) is the theoretical baseline, evicting the page used farthest in the future — unimplementable but useful for benchmarking.

chunks.title: Segmentation
chunks.text: Segmentation divides a process's address space into variable-size segments (code, data, heap, stack), each with a base and limit. Hardware checks limit on every access. Pure segmentation suffers external fragmentation. x86 historically combined segmentation with paging; modern systems use flat memory models with paging only, treating segment registers as a relic.

chunks.title: Disk scheduling
chunks.text: When multiple I/O requests queue for a disk, the order affects throughput. FCFS is simple but inefficient. SSTF (shortest seek time first) minimises seek time but can starve far-away requests. SCAN (elevator) sweeps the head across all tracks in one direction; C-SCAN does the same but only sweeps in one direction, jumping back. LOOK and C-LOOK are SCAN variants that turn around at the last request. SSDs make most of this obsolete — random access is essentially free.

chunks.title: File allocation methods
chunks.text: Contiguous allocation places a file in consecutive blocks — fast sequential reads, but fragmentation and growth issues. Linked allocation stores each block's next-pointer in the block — no external fragmentation, but random access is O(n). Indexed allocation uses an index block per file pointing to data blocks — random access in one extra read, used by ext2/3/4 with multi-level indexes for large files.

chunks.title: DMA and I/O
chunks.text: Direct Memory Access (DMA) lets devices read/write RAM without involving the CPU per byte. The CPU programs the DMA controller with source, destination, and length, then resumes work; an interrupt signals completion. DMA is essential for high-bandwidth I/O — disk, network cards, GPUs — where per-byte CPU involvement would bottleneck the system.

chunks.title: Microkernel vs monolithic
chunks.text: A monolithic kernel runs all OS services (drivers, file system, networking) in kernel space — fast (no IPC overhead) but a bug in one driver can crash the system. Linux and Windows NT are monolithic-with-modules. A microkernel runs only the minimum (IPC, scheduling, memory) in kernel space and pushes services to user space — more robust but slower due to IPC costs. MINIX 3 and seL4 are microkernels; macOS's XNU is a hybrid.

chunks.title: Real-time scheduling
chunks.text: Real-time systems must meet deadlines, not just run fast. Soft real-time tolerates occasional misses (video playback); hard real-time doesn't (avionics, medical pumps). Rate-Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) assigns priorities by period (shorter period = higher priority) and is optimal for static priorities. Earliest Deadline First (EDF) is optimal among dynamic-priority schedulers — admits any task set whose total utilization is ≤ 100%.

chunks.title: Boot process
chunks.text: When a computer powers on, firmware (BIOS or UEFI) runs POST, finds the boot device, and loads the bootloader (GRUB, Windows Boot Manager). The bootloader loads the kernel into memory and transfers control. The kernel initializes hardware, mounts the root filesystem, and starts the init process (PID 1, usually systemd on modern Linux). Init reads its configuration and launches services in dependency order.

chunks.title: Shell and command interpreter
chunks.text: A shell is a program that reads commands and executes them. It parses input into argv, performs expansion (variables, globs, command substitution), and forks/execs the requested program. Shells support pipelines (connect stdout to stdin), redirection (> < >>), background execution (&), and scripting. bash, zsh, fish are interactive shells; sh is the POSIX-compliant baseline. PowerShell streams objects, not text.

chunks.title: Banker's algorithm
chunks.text: The Banker's algorithm prevents deadlock by simulating each resource request to see if granting it would leave the system in a safe state — meaning all processes can still finish given current allocations. If safe, grant; otherwise, block. Requires advance knowledge of each process's maximum resource needs, which limits applicability. It's mostly a teaching example; real systems use prevention or detection.

chunks.title: Working set model
chunks.text: A process's working set at time t is the set of pages it referenced in the last Δ time units. The OS aims to keep each process's working set in RAM; processes whose working sets don't fit cause thrashing and may be suspended. Page-fault frequency (PFF) is a related approach — if a process's fault rate is too high, give it more frames; too low, take some away.

chunks.title: Linux process descriptor
chunks.text: Linux represents each process as a task_struct in the kernel, holding the PID, state, parent pointer, register snapshot, file descriptors, memory maps (mm_struct), and signal handlers. Tasks form a doubly-linked list and a tree by parent. The 'process' concept is unified with threads — a thread is a task that shares mm_struct (and optionally fds, signal handlers) with another, controlled by flags to clone().

chunks.title: Monitors
chunks.text: A monitor is a high-level synchronization construct bundling shared data with the procedures that access it. Only one thread can execute a monitor procedure at a time. Condition variables inside the monitor let threads wait until a predicate becomes true. Java's synchronized methods and wait/notify implement the monitor pattern. They simplify reasoning about concurrency by hiding the underlying mutex.

chunks.title: Spinlocks vs blocking locks
chunks.text: A spinlock busy-waits in a tight loop until the lock is free — appropriate when contention is rare and critical sections are short, since avoiding the context-switch cost wins. A blocking lock parks the thread and asks the scheduler to wake it when the lock is released — better when critical sections are long or contention high. Many implementations adaptive-spin first, then block.

chunks.title: Context switching cost
chunks.text: A context switch saves the current process's register state, swaps memory mappings, and loads the next process's state. Costs include direct CPU time (~1-5 µs) and indirect cache pollution — the new process's working set isn't in L1/L2, so it suffers cold misses. Avoiding switches matters: pinning threads to cores, batching I/O, and using async I/O reduce switches per unit of work.

chunks.title: Copy-on-write
chunks.text: Copy-on-write (COW) lets two processes share the same physical pages until one writes, at which point the kernel transparently makes a private copy for the writer. fork() uses COW so the child doesn't have to duplicate the parent's full address space upfront — most pages are never written, especially if exec follows fork quickly. COW is also used by file systems (Btrfs, ZFS, APFS) for snapshots.

chunks.title: Inverted page tables
chunks.text: A regular page table has one entry per virtual page per process — huge on 64-bit systems. An inverted page table has one entry per physical frame, indexed by frame number, listing which (process, virtual page) currently maps there. Lookup uses a hash. The savings are big but the lookup is slower, so most modern systems use multi-level page tables instead, with the TLB hiding the cost.

chunks.title: Process priorities and nice values
chunks.text: On Linux, every process has a nice value (-20 to 19; lower = higher priority, default 0). Only root can lower nice below 0. The Completely Fair Scheduler (CFS) uses nice to weight the share of CPU each task gets — a task with nice -5 gets roughly 1.25× the share of nice 0. Real-time scheduling classes (SCHED_FIFO, SCHED_RR) bypass CFS entirely and run at higher priority.

chunks.title: Signals
chunks.text: Signals are short, asynchronous notifications between processes (or from kernel to process). SIGINT (Ctrl+C) interrupts; SIGTERM politely asks to terminate; SIGKILL forces termination (uncatchable); SIGSEGV indicates segfault; SIGCHLD tells a parent its child changed state. A process installs handlers with signal() or sigaction(). Some handlers are restricted to async-signal-safe functions — write() yes, printf() no.
